CITY OF MILLBROOK
OFFICE OF THE CITY CLERK
PUBLIC RECORDS DIVISION

BUNDLE ID: ZBA-2024-06-01
SUBJECT: REGULAR MEETING MINUTES AND SUPPORTING DOCUMENTATION
DATE: JUNE 1, 2024
PAGE COUNT: 12 PAGES
STATUS: OFFICIAL RECORD

DOCUMENT COVER SHEET

This compilation contains public records from the Millbrook Zoning Board of Appeals regarding the Regular Meeting held on June 1, 2024. The following documents are included in this official bundle for distribution to all Board Members, City Clerk, and Public Record archives. The documents include the official meeting minutes, inter-departmental memoranda regarding logistics, campaign finance filings, property transfer records, environmental review summaries, and appendices containing public comment transcripts and staff reports. All documents are filed under the record date of June 1, 2024. The filing date for this bundle is June 1, 2024. The distribution list includes all Board Members, City Clerk, and relevant municipal departments.

The Zoning Board of Appeals convened at 6:00 PM. The meeting was called to order by Chairperson Patterson at 6:02 PM. A roll call was taken to establish a quorum. The meeting took place at City Hall Room 204. The ZBA Members present included Marcus Patterson, Elena Kowalski, David Rivera, Sarah Chen, Robert Thompson, and Linda Martinez. Planning Staff included Director James Wilson and Associate Planner Lisa Green. The City Clerk was John Davis. The meeting was recorded by the ZBA Administrative Assistant.

The following sections comprise the full administrative record for this session.

***

INTER-DEPARTMENTAL MEMORANDUM

TO: All Board Members, City Clerk
FROM: Millbrook Planning Department
DATE: May 28, 2024
SUBJECT: Logistics and Agenda Preparation for June 1, 2024 ZBA Meeting

This memorandum serves to confirm the logistical arrangements for the upcoming Regular Meeting of the Zoning Board of Appeals scheduled for June 1, 2024. The meeting location is confirmed as City Hall Room 204. The session is scheduled to begin at 6:00 PM. The agenda has been finalized and distributed to all Board Members and the City Clerk.

The agenda items are listed as follows:
Agenda Item 1: Variance 24-001
Agenda Item 2: Zoning Ordinance Amendment 24-02
Agenda Item 3: Public Comment

The Millbrook Planning Department has prepared the necessary materials for the hearing. Staff Report documentation is available for review. Site Plan documentation is available for review. The applicant, Thompson Residential LLC, has been notified of the hearing time and location. Public notification has been posted in accordance with municipal code requirements. The notice was posted at 1240 Oak Street.

The City Clerk, John Davis, is responsible for the official recording of the minutes. The ZBA Administrative Assistant is responsible for the distribution of the official record. The meeting is expected to adjourn by 6:45 PM. The next scheduled meeting is June 15, 2024.

Please review the attached Staff Report prior to the meeting. The Planning Director, James Wilson, is available to answer questions regarding the variance request. Associate Planner Lisa Green is available to provide technical specifications regarding the property lines.

This memorandum is part of the official administrative correspondence for the ZBA-2024-06-01 bundle. No further action is required from Board Members prior to the meeting. The City Clerk will maintain the distribution list. All Board Members are requested to review the documentation at least 24 hours prior to the convening of the board.

The Millbrook Planning Department maintains all records in compliance with state open meeting laws. The meeting location, City Hall Room 204, is accessible to the public. Seating is available for public comment. The public comment duration is set for 5 minutes per speaker. The number of speakers is limited to ensure the meeting concludes within the allotted time.

The administrative assistant will record the roll call. The roll call will verify the presence of a quorum. The quorum is established when a majority of the members are present. The ZBA Members present for the roll call are Marcus Patterson, Elena Kowalski, David Rivera, Sarah Chen, Robert Thompson, and Linda Martinez.

This memorandum is filed under the subject of Regular Meeting Minutes - June 1, 2024. The document status is Official Record. The page count for this memorandum is one page. The filing date is June 1, 2024. The distribution list includes all Board Members and City Clerk.

***

4. Traffic Patterns: No significant impact.
5. Wildlife Habitat: No significant impact.

REVIEW PROCESS
The review was conducted in accordance with the National Environmental Policy Act. The review was completed on June 1, 2024. The reviewer was Associate Planner Lisa Green. The reviewer was Director James Wilson. The review was filed with the City Clerk. The City Clerk is John Davis. The review was distributed to all Board Members. The distribution list includes all Board Members, City Clerk. The document status is Official Record. The page count for this summary is 2 pages. The attachment includes the site plan.

The variance type is Side Setback 2.5 feet. The setback variance is within standard tolerance. The Planning Director confirmed that the setback variance is within standard tolerance. The meeting was called to order by Chairperson Patterson at 6:02 PM. The board convened at 6:00 PM. The roll call was taken. The quorum was established.

AGENDA ITEM 1: VARIANCE 24-001
The Board considered the variance request for Parcel ID: 14-05-22-100. The address is 1240 Oak Street. The applicant is Thompson Residential LLC. The variance type is Side Setback 2.5 feet. The Staff Recommendation is APPROVE.

The Planning Director, James Wilson, presented the staff report. The Planning Director confirmed that the setback variance is within standard tolerance. Member Wilson noted that the applicant provided all required documentation. The applicant, Thompson Residential LLC, was present. Robert Thompson was present.

The Board reviewed the Site Plan. The Site Plan is Attachment 1. The Staff Report is Attachment 2. The Sign-in Sheet is Attachment 3. The Filing date is June 1, 2024. The Distribution list includes all Board Members, City Clerk. The Document status is Official Record. The Page count is 12 pages.

Discussion:
Chairperson Patterson asked for clarification on the side setback measurement. Associate Planner Lisa Green provided the measurement. The measurement was 2.5 feet. The standard tolerance was reviewed. The standard tolerance was met. The Millbrook Planning Department reviewed the property lines. The property lines were verified. The property lines match the legal description. The legal description is Lot 4, Block 12, Oak Street Subdivision.

Motion:
A motion was made to approve variance 24-001. The motion was made by Member Rivera. The motion was seconded by Member Chen. The motion was to approve. The motion was carried.

Vote:
The vote was recorded. The vote tally was 6 for, 0 against, 1 abstention. The motion to approve variance 24-001 was carried 6-0.

AGENDA ITEM 2: ZONING ORDINANCE AMENDMENT 24-02

The amendment relates to the square footage. The amendment relates to the lot size. The amendment relates to the zoning classification.

The ordinance text was distributed to the City Clerk. The City Clerk is John Davis. The City Clerk distributed the ordinance text.

Discussion:
Member Kowalski asked for clarification on the zoning classification. Member Kowalski asked for clarification on the R-1 Residential classification. The Planning Director provided the clarification. The Planning Director confirmed the classification. The classification is R-1 Residential. The classification is accurate. The classification is within standard tolerance.

Member Martinez asked for clarification on the square footage. Member Martinez asked for clarification on the lot size. The Associate Planner provided the clarification. The Associate Planner confirmed the square footage. The Associate Planner confirmed the lot size. The square footage is 7,500 square feet. The lot size is 0.17 acres.

Motion:
A motion was made to approve Zoning Ordinance Amendment 24-02. The motion was made by Member Thompson. The motion was seconded by Member Patterson. The motion was to approve. The motion was carried.

Vote:
The vote was recorded. The vote tally was 6 for, 0 against, 1 abstention. The motion to approve Zoning Ordinance Amendment 24-02 was carried 6-0.

ADJOURNMENT

The next scheduled meeting is open to the public. The next scheduled meeting is recorded by the ZBA Administrative Assistant.

**DOCUMENT A: OFFICIAL MINUTES OF THE NORTHWOOD INDEPENDENT SCHOOL DISTRICT BOARD OF TRUSTEES REGULAR MEETING HELD ON SEPTEMBER 15, 2023**

**CALL TO ORDER**

The Regular Meeting of the Board of Trustees of the Northwood Independent School District was called to order by President J. Anderson at 6:00 p.m. in the District Administrative Center, Board Room A, located at 1200 Education Boulevard, Northwood, Texas.

**ROLL CALL**

The following members of the Board of Trustees were present:
J. Anderson (President)
M. Chen (Vice President)
R. Davis
L. Foster
K. Gupta
T. Harris

The following district officials were present:
Dr. Elena Rostova, Superintendent of Schools
Mr. Thomas Halloway, Chief Financial Officer
Ms. Sarah Jenkins, Board Secretary
Mr. David Miller, Legal Counsel

A quorum was declared present and the meeting was deemed open for the transaction of business.

**INVOCATION AND PLEDGE OF ALLEGIANCE**

The invocation was offered by Board Member L. Foster. The Pledge of Allegiance was recited by the Board and attendees, led by Superintendent Rostova.

**APPROVAL OF AGENDA**

A motion was made by Board Member R. Davis and seconded by Board Member K. Gupta to approve the agenda for the September 15, 2023, meeting as distributed.
Motion Carried: 6-0.

**PUBLIC COMMENT – GENERAL**

The floor was opened for general public comment. Three individuals signed up to speak.

1.  **Mr. Arthur Penhaligon**, representing the Northwood Teachers Association, addressed the Board. Mr. Penhaligon stated that the upcoming budget cycle is critical for maintaining current staffing levels. He requested that the Board ensure the "Instructional Salaries" line item reflects the cost-of-living adjustments previously discussed in the fiscal year planning committee.

2.  **Ms. Linda Vargo**, a parent of a student in the Northwood High School District, spoke regarding facility maintenance. She expressed concern that budget allocations for non-instructional areas might be insufficient given the age of the district's infrastructure.

3.  **Mr. James Kowalski**, a local business owner, offered support for the proposed budget, noting the district's role as a major employer in the region.

Following the public comment period, the Board Chair thanked the speakers and noted that all comments would be added to the official record.

**CONSENT AGENDA**

A motion was made by Board Member M. Chen and seconded by Board Member T. Harris to approve the Consent Agenda, which included:
1.  Approval of the minutes from the August 18, 2023, Regular Meeting.
2.  Approval of the August 2023 Payroll Register.
3.  Approval of the September 2023 Vendor Payments totaling $4,250,000.00.
4.  Approval of the transfer of $150,000.00 from the Technology Reserve Fund to the General Fund for immediate server upgrades.

Discussion was requested regarding item 4. Mr. Thomas Halloway, Chief Financial Officer, provided a brief explanation of the server capacity issues and the necessity of the transfer.

A motion was made to separate Item 4 from the Consent Agenda for further discussion.
Motion to Separate: Carried.

The remaining items on the Consent Agenda were voted upon.
Motion to Approve Consent Agenda (excluding Item 4): Carried.

**AGENDA ITEM 1: CONSIDERATION OF FY2024 BUDGET PROPOSAL**

**Presentation by Superintendent**
Superintendent Dr. Elena Rostova called the meeting to order for the primary agenda item. She introduced Mr. Thomas Halloway, Chief Financial Officer, to present the preliminary financial statements for the Fiscal Year 2024.

Mr. Halloway presented a comprehensive overview of the proposed budget. He highlighted that the total budget for the upcoming fiscal year is set at $145,000,000. This figure represents a 4.2% increase over the FY2023 actuals, primarily driven by inflationary pressures in the energy sector and the mandatory increase in instructional salary scales.

Mr. Halloway directed the Board's attention to the Operating Expenses section of the financial statement. He specifically noted the line item for "Instructional Salaries," which is proposed at $98,500,000. He stated that this amount constitutes 68% of the total budget, maintaining the district's long-standing commitment to prioritizing direct classroom instruction over administrative overhead.

Mr. Halloway detailed the breakdown of the $98,500,000 allocation:
*   Base Salaries: $72,000,000
*   Step Increases: $12,500,000
*   Longevity Pay: $4,000,000
*   Special Education Incentives: $3,500,000
*   Teacher Retirement System Contributions: $6,500,000

He further explained that the remaining 32% of the budget is allocated to facilities maintenance, transportation, food services, technology infrastructure, and administrative operations.

**Discussion**
Board Member R. Davis asked for clarification on the 68% allocation to instructional salaries. Mr. Halloway responded that this percentage is consistent with the district's strategic plan goals to maximize classroom resources. He noted that a reduction in this percentage would require a corresponding reduction in instructional staff or an increase in class sizes, neither of which is recommended by the district's strategic planning committee.

Board Member L. Foster inquired about the contingency funds included in the budget. Mr. Halloway stated that a contingency reserve of $2.5 million is included within the general fund, to be utilized only in the event of significant revenue shortfalls or unforeseen emergency repairs.

Board Member K. Gupta questioned the impact of the proposed budget on the district's credit rating. Mr. Halloway assured the Board that the financial structure remains sound, with a debt service coverage ratio well above the required threshold for municipal bond issuance.

Board Member T. Harris raised a concern regarding the timing of the budget adoption relative to the start of the school year. Superintendent Rostova confirmed that the budget must be adopted by September 30, 2023, to ensure all payroll systems are updated for the October 1st fiscal year start.

**MOTION TO ADOPT BUDGET**
Superintendent Rostova moved to adopt the FY2024 Budget Proposal as presented, totaling $145,000,000, with the specific allocation of $98,500,000 to Instructional Salaries, representing 68% of the total budget.

A second was made by Board Member M. Chen.

The Board proceeded to a roll-call vote on the motion to adopt the FY2024 Budget.

**ROLL CALL VOTE**

1.  **J. Anderson (President):** Aye
2.  **M. Chen (Vice President):** Aye
3.  **R. Davis:** Aye
4.  **L. Foster:** Nay
5.  **K. Gupta:** Aye
6.  **T. Harris:** Nay

**VOTE RESULT**
The motion carried with a vote count of 5-2.

**ADJOURNMENT**
There being no further business, a motion was made by Board Member R. Davis and seconded by Board Member K. Gupta to adjourn the meeting.
Motion Carried.
The meeting was adjourned at 7:45 p.m.

**CERTIFICATION**

**DOCUMENT B: FY2024 OPERATING EXPENSES FINANCIAL STATEMENT**

**NORTHWOOD INDEPENDENT SCHOOL DISTRICT**
**COMPREHENSIVE ANNUAL FINANCIAL REPORT**
**FISCAL YEAR 2024 PROPOSED BUDGET**
**PREPARED BY: OFFICE OF FINANCIAL PLANNING AND ANALYSIS**
**DATE: SEPTEMBER 1, 2023**

**1.0 EXECUTIVE SUMMARY**
The following financial statement outlines the proposed operating expenses for the Northwood Independent School District for the Fiscal Year 2024. The total budgeted amount for the district is established at $145,000,000. This document serves as the official record of the financial allocation decisions made by the Board of Trustees on September 15, 2023.

**2.0 SUMMARY OF REVENUES**
Total Estimated Revenues: $145,000,000
*   State Foundation School Program: $92,000,000
*   Local Property Taxes: $45,000,000
*   Federal Grants: $5,500,000
*   Other Revenue Sources: $2,500,000

**3.0 SUMMARY OF EXPENDITURES**
Total Expenditures: $145,000,000

**3.1 INSTRUCTIONAL DEPARTMENT**
The Instructional Department represents the core function of the district. The total allocation for this department is $105,200,000.

**3.1.1 Instructional Salaries**
*   **Line Item:** Instructional Salaries
*   **Amount:** $98,500,000
*   **Percentage of Budget:** 68%
*   **Description:** This line item covers all compensation for certified instructional staff, including teachers, counselors, librarians, and instructional coaches. It includes base salaries, step increases based on years of service, and longevity pay.
*   **Year-over-Year Change:** +4.5%

**3.1.2 Instructional Supplies and Materials**
*   **Amount:** $4,200,000
*   **Description:** Textbooks, digital learning resources, laboratory supplies, and classroom consumables.

**3.1.3 Professional Development**
*   **Amount:** $1,800,000
*   **Description:** Training costs, conference fees, and substitute teacher coverage for professional learning communities.

**3.2 SUPPORT SERVICES DEPARTMENT**
*   **Total Allocation:** $28,500,000
*   **Breakdown:**
    *   Special Education Services: $12,000,000
    *   Student Services (Counseling, Nursing, Psychology): $5,500,000
    *   Curriculum and Instruction Support: $3,500,000
    *   Transportation: $4,500,000
    *   Food Services: $3,000,000

**3.3 ADMINISTRATIVE DEPARTMENT**
*   **Total Allocation:** $8,000,000
*   **Breakdown:**
    *   District Administration: $4,000,000
    *   Board of Trustees Expenses: $150,000
    *   Legal and Audit Fees: $800,000
    *   Human Resources: $1,200,000
    *   Information Technology (General Admin): $1,850,000

**3.4 FACILITIES AND CAPITAL OUTLAY**
*   **Total Allocation:** $3,300,000
*   **Breakdown:**
    *   Maintenance and Repair: $2,500,000
    *   Utilities: $600,000
    *   Capital Improvements: $200,000

**4.0 NOTES TO FINANCIAL STATEMENTS**

**4.1 Instructional Salaries Analysis**
The "Instructional Salaries" line item at $98,500,000 represents the single largest expenditure in the district's operating budget. This figure accounts for 68% of the total $145,000,000 budget. The allocation is based on the following headcount projections:
*   Elementary Teachers: 450 FTE
*   Middle School Teachers: 320 FTE
*   High School Teachers: 380 FTE
*   Special Education Instructors: 150 FTE
*   Instructional Support Staff: 50 FTE

The average salary per FTE is calculated at approximately $105,882, inclusive of benefits and retirement contributions. This average reflects the district's commitment to competitive compensation to attract and retain high-quality educators.

**4.2 Budgetary Controls**
All expenditures are subject to the district's internal control policies. Any expenditure exceeding the allocated line item by more than 5% requires prior approval from the Board of Trustees. The Chief Financial Officer is authorized to make transfers between line items within the same department up to a maximum of $250,000 without Board approval, provided the total departmental budget is not exceeded.

**4.3 Compliance**
This financial statement has been prepared in accordance with the Governmental Accounting Standards Board (GASB) Statement No. 34 and the Texas Education Agency reporting requirements.

**DOCUMENT F: ENVIRONMENTAL REVIEW SUMMARY**

**NORTHWOOD INDEPENDENT SCHOOL DISTRICT**
**OFFICE OF ENVIRONMENTAL COMPLIANCE**
**ENVIRONMENTAL REVIEW SUMMARY**
**PROJECT:** NORTHWOOD EAST MIDDLE SCHOOL EXPANSION
**DATE:** SEPTEMBER 15, 2023
**PROJECT ID:** ENV-2023-0915-001

**1.0 PROJECT DESCRIPTION**
The Northwood Independent School District proposes to acquire Parcel ID NW-740-12-B located at 4500 Northwood East Road. The purpose of the acquisition is to construct a 40,000 square foot academic wing and associated athletic facilities for the Northwood East Middle School campus. The total cost of the project is estimated at $12,500,000, funded by a combination of general fund reserves and state construction grants.

**2.0 ENVIRONMENTAL ASSESSMENT**
A preliminary environmental assessment was conducted on September 1, 2023, by the District's Environmental Compliance Officer. The assessment focused on the following areas:

**2.1 Land Use and Zoning**
The parcel is currently zoned as "Commercial/Industrial" by the City of Northwood. The School District has filed a rezoning application to change the zoning to "Educational/Institutional." The application is currently under review by the City Planning Commission. The proposed use is consistent with the Comprehensive Plan for the Northwood East area.

**2.2 Soil and Water Quality**
Soil sampling was conducted on August 25, 2023. The results indicate no significant contamination. The soil is suitable for construction. The site is located outside of the 100-year floodplain. A stormwater management plan has been developed to ensure compliance with the Texas Commission on Environmental Quality (TCEQ) regulations.

**2.3 Air Quality**
The construction phase is expected to generate minimal air pollution. Dust control measures will be implemented during excavation. The proposed building design includes energy-efficient HVAC systems to reduce emissions.

**2.4 Biological Resources**
A biological survey was conducted on August 20, 2023. No endangered species were identified on the site. The area contains native grasses and a few mature oak trees. A mitigation plan has been developed to preserve 30% of the existing tree canopy.

**3.0 PUBLIC NOTICE**
Public notice of the environmental review was published in the Northwood Gazette on September 1, 2023. A 30-day comment period was established. No adverse comments were received regarding the environmental impact of the project.

**4.0 CONCLUSIONS**
Based on the environmental assessment, the project is deemed to have a "No Significant Impact" on the environment. The project complies with all federal, state, and local environmental regulations.

**5.0 RECOMMENDATIONS**
It is recommended that the Board of Trustees approve the acquisition of Parcel NW-740-12-B and proceed with the construction of the new academic wing. The Environmental Compliance Officer will continue to monitor the project to ensure compliance with all environmental conditions.

**6.0 APPROVALS**

**Dr. Elena Rostova**
Superintendent of Schools
Northwood Independent School District
Date: September 15, 2023

**Mr. David Miller**
Legal Counsel
Northwood Independent School District

**DOCUMENT G: INTER-DEPARTMENTAL MEMOS**

**NORTHWOOD INDEPENDENT SCHOOL DISTRICT**
**INTERNAL MEMORANDUM**

**TO:** Board of Trustees
**FROM:** Thomas Halloway, Chief Financial Officer
**DATE:** September 15, 2023
**SUBJECT:** FY2024 Budget Approval and Fiscal Year Transition

**1.0 PURPOSE**
The purpose of this memorandum is to provide a summary of the FY2024 budget approval process and to outline the immediate steps required for the transition to the new fiscal year.

**2.0 BACKGROUND**
The Board of Trustees met on September 15, 2023, to review and vote on the proposed FY2024 budget. The total budget was approved at $145,000,000. A key component of this budget is the allocation of $98,500,000 for Instructional Salaries, which represents 68% of the total budget. The vote count was 5-2 in favor of the budget.

**3.0 BUDGET IMPLEMENTATION PLAN**
The following actions are required to implement the approved budget:

*   **Payroll System Update:** The Human Resources department will update the payroll system to reflect the new salary scales effective October 1, 2023.
*   **Vendor Contracts:** The Procurement department will review all active vendor contracts to ensure they align with the new budget allocations.
*   **Grant Applications:** The Grants department will submit applications for state and federal funding based on the approved budget figures.
*   **Facilities Maintenance:** The Facilities department will begin the planning phase for the Northwood East Middle School expansion using the allocated funds.

**4.0 FINANCIAL CONTROLS**
The Finance department will implement the following controls to ensure compliance with the budget:
*   Monthly budget vs. actual reports will be generated for each department.
*   Any expenditure exceeding the budgeted amount by more than 5% will require Board approval.
*   The Chief Financial Officer will conduct a mid-year review of the budget to identify any variances.

**5.0 COMMUNICATION**
The Communications department will prepare a press release to inform the public of the budget approval. The release will highlight the district's commitment to instructional salaries and the total budget figure of $145,000,000.

**6.0 CONCLUSION**
The approval of the FY2024 budget marks a significant milestone for the Northwood Independent School District. The Board's decision to allocate 68% of the budget to Instructional Salaries demonstrates a strong commitment to the district's primary mission. The Finance department is committed to managing these funds responsibly and ensuring a smooth transition to the new fiscal year.

**CC:** Superintendent of Schools, Board Secretary, Department Heads

**INTERNAL MEMORANDUM**

**TO:** Board of Trustees
**FROM:** Dr. Elena Rostova, Superintendent of Schools
**DATE:** September 15, 2023
**SUBJECT:** Superintendent's Report on FY2024 Budget

**1.0 PURPOSE**
The purpose of this memorandum is to provide the Board of Trustees with a summary of the Superintendent's report on the FY2024 budget.

**2.0 BACKGROUND**
The Superintendent's Office has worked closely with the Finance Department to develop the FY2024 budget. The budget reflects the district's commitment to providing a high-quality education for all students.

**3.0 KEY POINTS**
*   **Instructional Focus:** The budget prioritizes Instructional Salaries, allocating $98,500,000, which is 68% of the total budget.
*   **Facilities:** The budget includes funds for the expansion of the Northwood East Middle School.
*   **Technology:** The budget includes funds for the purchase of 2,500 laptops for student use.
*   **Equity:** The budget includes funds for special education and English Language Learner programs.

**4.0 CONCLUSION**
The FY2024 budget is a balanced and responsible plan that addresses the needs of the district. The Board's approval of the budget will ensure the continued success of the Northwood Independent School District.

**5.0 RECOMMENDATION**
It is recommended that the Board of Trustees adopt the FY2024 budget as presented.

ITEM 7: ADVERTISEMENT FOR BIDS – DOWNTOWN STREET RESURFACING PROJECT

Planning Director Elena R. Rossi presented the proposal for the Downtown Street Resurfacing Project. The project encompasses the resurfacing of Main Street from 1st Avenue to 5th Avenue, including the installation of new curb ramps, repainting of crosswalks, and the replacement of three sections of storm drain grates. The total estimated cost is $425,000. The funding source is the State Transportation Improvement Grant (STIG) 2023-2024, with a local match of 10%.

Staff Recommendation: The Planning Department recommends that the City Council authorize the advertisement for bids for the Downtown Street Resurfacing Project. The advertisement will be published in the Millbrook Gazette and the Millbrook Daily Chronicle for a period of two weeks. Bids will be accepted until 2:00 P.M. on July 15, 2024. The contract will be awarded to the lowest responsible bidder, subject to final engineering review and approval by the City Attorney.

Public Comment:
Council Member Miller asked if the project timeline accounts for the upcoming summer festival on Main Street.
Director Rossi responded that the project schedule has been coordinated with the Festival Committee. Construction is scheduled to begin on August 1, 2024, immediately following the conclusion of the festival.
Council Member O'Malley inquired about the material specifications for the asphalt mix.
Director Rossi stated that the specifications call for a polymer-modified asphalt mix to ensure durability and reduce noise levels, in compliance with the State Department of Transportation standards.
Council Member Thompson asked about the contingency fund allocation.
Director Rossi noted that the bid estimate includes a 5% contingency fund, which is standard for municipal infrastructure projects of this scope.

Motion by Council Member Chen, seconded by Council Member O'Malley, to authorize the advertisement for bids for the Downtown Street Resurfacing Project, Contract No. 2024-045, with a total estimated cost not to exceed $425,000, subject to final engineering review.

Roll Call Vote:
Council Member Vance: Aye
Council Member Chen: Aye
Council Member Miller: Aye
Council Member O'Malley: Aye
Council Member Thompson: Aye

Motion Carried.

ITEM 11: ZONING BOARD OF APPEALS HEARING – VARIANCE REQUEST FOR 450 RIVER ROAD

Planning Director Elena R. Rossi introduced the matter regarding a variance request submitted by the owner of 450 River Road, a property currently zoned R-2 (Single Family Residential). The applicant, Mr. James P. Henderson, is seeking a variance to allow the construction of a two-story accessory dwelling unit (ADU) and a detached garage structure that exceeds the current setback requirements by 4.5 feet. The proposed structure is to be used for short-term rental purposes, subject to the City's short-term rental ordinance.

The applicant states that the property is located in a high-demand area for short-term rentals due to its proximity to the Millbrook River Trail. The applicant argues that the variance is necessary to maximize the utility of the property and that the structure will not negatively impact the character of the neighborhood. The applicant has submitted a traffic impact study, an architectural rendering, and a letter of support from the immediate neighbors.

Staff Recommendation: The Planning Department recommends the denial of the variance request. The staff report notes that the proposed setback variance of 4.5 feet does not meet the criteria for a variance under Section 14.05 of the Millbrook Zoning Ordinance. The staff argues that the property is not unique in its physical characteristics and that the hardship is self-imposed. Furthermore, the staff notes that the proposed use as a short-term rental may create a nuisance for the surrounding residential area.

Zoning Board Administrator Marcus T. Webb stated that the Zoning Board of Appeals will hear the case on June 18, 2024. The Board will review the application, hear testimony from the applicant and the public, and render a decision. The Board may approve the variance with conditions, deny the variance, or remand the application for further review.

Public Comment:
Council Member Vance asked if the applicant has addressed the noise concerns raised in the traffic impact study.
Director Rossi responded that the applicant has proposed a sound barrier wall along the property line, which is included in the architectural rendering.
Council Member O'Malley asked if the short-term rental license has been issued.
Director Rossi stated that the short-term rental license has not been issued as the variance is a prerequisite for the license.
Council Member Thompson asked if the neighbors have signed the letter of support.
Director Rossi confirmed that the letter of support was signed by the owners of 448 and 452 River Road.

Motion by Council Member Chen, seconded by Council Member Miller, to forward the variance request for 450 River Road to the Zoning Board of Appeals for a hearing on June 18, 2024, and to direct the Planning Department to prepare a comprehensive staff report for the hearing.

Roll Call Vote:
Council Member Vance: Aye
Council Member Chen: Aye
Council Member Miller: Aye
Council Member O'Malley: Aye
Council Member Thompson: Aye

Motion Carried.

ITEM 12: ENVIRONMENTAL REVIEW SUMMARY – MILLBROOK WASTEWATER TREATMENT PLANT UPGRADE

Public Works Director James P. Garris presented the Environmental Review Summary for the Millbrook Wastewater Treatment Plant Upgrade Project. The project involves the installation of new filtration systems, the expansion of the sludge dewatering facility, and the upgrade of the disinfection system to comply with the new State Environmental Protection Standards. The total estimated cost is $2,850,000. The funding source is a combination of State Environmental Improvement Grants and City General Fund reserves.

The Environmental Impact Statement (EIS) was completed on May 15, 2024. The EIS concluded that the project would have a minimal impact on the local ecosystem. The primary concerns addressed in the EIS were potential air quality impacts during construction and the disposal of sludge. The EIS recommends the implementation of dust control measures and the use of a certified waste disposal facility for sludge.

Staff Recommendation: The Public Works Department recommends the approval of the Environmental Review Summary and the authorization to proceed with the design phase of the project. The design phase is expected to take six months. Construction is scheduled to begin in January 2025.

Public Comment:
Council Member Miller asked about the timeline for the sludge disposal facility certification.
Director Garris responded that the certification process is underway and is expected to be completed by August 2024.
Council Member O'Malley asked if the dust control measures have been budgeted.
Director Garris confirmed that dust control measures are included in the construction budget.
Council Member Thompson asked about the public notification process for the EIS.
Director Garris stated that the EIS was published in the Millbrook Gazette and available for public review at the City Library and the Public Works Department office for a period of 30 days. No public comments were received during the review period.

Motion by Council Member Chen, seconded by Council Member O'Malley, to approve the Environmental Review Summary for the Millbrook Wastewater Treatment Plant Upgrade Project and to authorize the design phase.

Roll Call Vote:
Council Member Vance: Aye
Council Member Chen: Aye
Council Member Miller: Aye
Council Member O'Malley: Aye
Council Member Thompson: Aye

Motion Carried.

ITEM 15: INTER-DEPARTMENTAL MEMO – COORDINATION OF SUMMER EVENTS

City Manager Richard D. Sterling presented an Inter-Departmental Memo regarding the coordination of summer events in Millbrook. The memo outlines the responsibilities of various departments for the upcoming summer festival, the farmers market, and the outdoor concert series.

The memo details the following:
- Public Works Department: Responsible for setting up stages, lighting, and sound equipment.
- Police Department: Responsible for security, traffic control, and emergency response.
- Parks and Recreation Department: Responsible for vendor coordination, food service, and activity planning.
- Planning Department: Responsible for permit issuance and zoning compliance.
- Finance Department: Responsible for budget oversight and revenue collection.
- Communications Department: Responsible for public relations and media outreach.

The memo emphasizes the need for close coordination between departments to ensure the success of the events. A joint planning meeting is scheduled for June 10, 2024, to finalize the details.

Council Member Chen asked about the budget allocation for the summer events.
City Manager Sterling responded that the budget for the summer events is $150,000, which is funded by the City's Special Events Fund.
Council Member O'Malley asked about the contingency plan for inclement weather.
City Manager Sterling stated that the contingency plan includes the availability of indoor venues and the postponement of events in case of severe weather.
Council Member Thompson asked about the vendor selection process.
City Manager Sterling responded that vendors are selected through a competitive bidding process, with priority given to local businesses.

Motion by Council Member Chen, seconded by Council Member Miller, to approve the Inter-Departmental Memo regarding the coordination of summer events and to schedule the joint planning meeting for June 10, 2024.

Roll Call Vote:
Council Member Vance: Aye
Council Member Chen: Aye

ITEM 2: VARIANCE REQUEST FOR 450 RIVER ROAD

Zoning Board Administrator Marcus T. Webb introduced the variance request for 450 River Road. The applicant, Mr. James P. Henderson, is seeking a variance to allow the construction of a two-story accessory dwelling unit (ADU) and a detached garage structure that exceeds the current setback requirements by 4.5 feet. The proposed structure is to be used for short-term rental purposes.

The applicant presented the case, highlighting the property's location in a high-demand area for short-term rentals and the architectural design of the proposed structure. The applicant submitted a traffic impact study, an architectural rendering, and a letter of support from the immediate neighbors.

The Planning Director presented the staff report, recommending the denial of the variance request. The staff report noted that the proposed setback variance of 4.5 feet does not meet the criteria for a variance under Section 14.05 of the Millbrook Zoning Ordinance. The staff argued that the property is not unique in its physical characteristics and that the hardship is self-imposed. Furthermore, the staff noted that the proposed use as a short-term rental may create a nuisance for the surrounding residential area.

Public Comment:
Mr. James P. Henderson, the applicant, spoke in support of the variance. He stated that the variance is necessary to maximize the utility of the property and that the structure will not negatively impact the character of the neighborhood. He emphasized the economic benefits of the short-term rental and the architectural design of the proposed structure.
Ms. John D. Smith, a resident of 450 River Road, spoke against the variance. She expressed concern about the potential increase in traffic and noise. She requested that the Board deny the variance.
Ms. Sarah L. Johnson, the Vice Chairperson, asked the applicant if he had addressed the noise concerns raised in the traffic impact study.
Mr. Henderson responded that he had proposed a sound barrier wall along the property line, which is included in the architectural rendering.
Member Davis asked if the neighbors had signed the letter of support.
Mr. Henderson confirmed that the letter of support was signed by the owners of 448 and 452 River Road.

Board Discussion:
Chairperson Miller asked the Board members to consider the criteria for a variance under Section 14.05 of the Millbrook Zoning Ordinance.
Vice Chairperson Johnson noted that the property is not unique in its physical characteristics.
Member Smith noted that the hardship is self-imposed.
Member Brown noted that the proposed use as a short-term rental may create a nuisance for the surrounding residential area.
Member Davis noted that the applicant has proposed a sound barrier wall, but the variance is still not justified.

Motion by Member Smith, seconded by Member Brown, to deny the variance request for 450 River Road.

Roll Call Vote:
Chairperson Miller: Aye
Vice Chairperson Johnson: Aye
Member Smith: Aye
Member Brown: Aye
Member Davis: Aye

Motion Carried.

INTER-DEPARTMENTAL MEMO
DATE: JUNE 4, 2024
TO: ALL DEPARTMENT HEADS
FROM: Richard D. Sterling, City Manager
SUBJECT: COORDINATION OF SUMMER EVENTS

This memo outlines the responsibilities of various departments for the upcoming summer festival, the farmers market, and the outdoor concert series.

DEPARTMENTAL RESPONSIBILITIES:
1. Public Works Department: Responsible for setting up stages, lighting, and sound equipment.
2. Police Department: Responsible for security, traffic control, and emergency response.
3. Parks and Recreation Department: Responsible for vendor coordination, food service, and activity planning.
4. Planning Department: Responsible for permit issuance and zoning compliance.
5. Finance Department: Responsible for budget oversight and revenue collection.
6. Communications Department: Responsible for public relations and media outreach.

The memo emphasizes the need for close coordination between departments to ensure the success of the events. A joint planning meeting is scheduled for June 10, 2024, to finalize the details.

BUDGET ALLOCATION: $150,000 (Funded by the City's Special Events Fund).
CONTINGENCY PLAN: Availability of indoor venues and postponement of events in case of severe weather.
VENDOR SELECTION PROCESS: Competitive bidding process, with priority given to local businesses.

NOTES:
1. The joint planning meeting is scheduled for June 10, 2024.
2. The budget is $150,000.
3. The contingency plan includes the availability of indoor venues and the postponement of events in case of severe weather.
4. The vendor selection process is a competitive bidding process, with priority given to local businesses.

NORTHWOOD INDEPENDENT SCHOOL DISTRICT
BOARD OF TRUSTEES REGULAR MEETING MINUTES
DATE: OCTOBER 12, 2023
TIME: 6:00 P.M.
LOCATION: DISTRICT ADMINISTRATION BUILDING, ROOM 101, NORTHWOOD, TEXAS

PRESIDING OFFICER: PRESIDENT M. THOMPSON
SECRETARY: J. HARRIS

CALL TO ORDER

The meeting was called to order at 6:00 P.M. by President M. Thompson.

ROLL CALL

Trustee M. Thompson was present.
Trustee L. Garcia was present.
Trustee K. O’Neill was present.
Trustee D. Chen was present.
Trustee A. Johnson was present.
Superintendent R. Wilson was present.
Legal Counsel J. Miller was present.
Union Representative S. Martinez was present.

A quorum was present.

PLEDGE OF ALLEGIANCE

The meeting commenced with the Pledge of Allegiance to the Flag of the United States of America, led by Superintendent R. Wilson.

APPROVAL OF AGENDA

Motion to approve the agenda was made by Trustee L. Garcia and seconded by Trustee K. O’Neill.
The roll call vote on the agenda approval was as follows:
Trustee M. Thompson: Aye
Trustee L. Garcia: Aye
Trustee K. O’Neill: Aye
Trustee D. Chen: Aye
Trustee A. Johnson: Aye

The agenda was approved unanimously.

APPROVAL OF PREVIOUS MEETING MINUTES

Minutes from the meeting held on September 14, 2023, were reviewed by the Board.
Motion to approve the minutes was made by Trustee D. Chen and seconded by Trustee A. Johnson.
The roll call vote on the approval of minutes was as follows:
Trustee M. Thompson: Aye
Trustee L. Garcia: Aye
Trustee K. O’Neill: Aye
Trustee D. Chen: Aye
Trustee A. Johnson: Aye

The minutes were approved.

SUPERINTENDENT REPORT

Superintendent R. Wilson presented the district report. The report included enrollment figures for the current fiscal year, facility usage statistics, and the current status of the capital improvement plan. The report noted that enrollment has stabilized at 12,500 students. Facility usage is at 94% capacity during peak hours. The capital improvement plan is on schedule for the completion of the Northwood High School roof replacement project scheduled for the spring of 2024.

CONSENT AGENDA

Item 7.1: Approval of vendor invoices for maintenance services.
Item 7.2: Approval of transportation route changes for elementary zones.
Item 7.3: Approval of new faculty employment contracts for the 2023-2024 academic year.

Motion to approve the consent agenda was made by Trustee L. Garcia and seconded by Trustee K. O’Neill.
The roll call vote on the consent agenda was as follows:
Trustee M. Thompson: Aye
Trustee L. Garcia: Aye
Trustee K. O’Neill: Aye
Trustee D. Chen: Aye
Trustee A. Johnson: Aye

The consent agenda was approved.

PUBLIC COMMENT PERIOD (GENERAL)

The public comment period was opened at 6:15 P.M.
Three members of the public signed the registration log.
Speaker 1: Resident of Northwood, Parcel ID 45-001. Comment regarding noise levels on North Street.
Speaker 2: Parent of student, District ID 998-22. Comment regarding bus pickup times.
Speaker 3: Resident of Northwood, Parcel ID 12-005. Comment regarding library hours.

The public comment period closed at 6:30 P.M.

NEW BUSINESS: CONTRACT RATIFICATION

Item 8.1: Discussion and Vote on Teacher Union Contract Ratification.

Superintendent R. Wilson took the podium to present the district position. The Superintendent outlined the budgetary constraints and the necessity of maintaining competitive compensation to retain qualified faculty. The Superintendent stated that the proposed agreement includes a salary increase of 4.5% over the current scale.

Union Representative S. Martinez took the podium to present the union position. The Representative outlined the demands regarding working conditions, class size caps, and compensation. The Representative stated that the proposed agreement secures a term of 3 Years.

Legal Counsel J. Miller provided a legal review of the contract language. The counsel confirmed that the contract language complies with state education code provisions regarding collective bargaining. The counsel noted the effective date is set for 2024-07-01.

Public Comment on Item 8.1 was opened.
The public comment period was scheduled for 45 minutes.
The clock was started at 6:35 P.M.

Public Comment Log Summary:
1.  J. Doe, 123 Main St. Comment: Support for the increase.
2.  M. Roe, 456 Oak Ave. Comment: Concerns regarding property tax implications.
3.  P. Smith, 789 Elm Rd. Comment: Support for the term length.
4.  T. White, 321 Pine Ln. Comment: Concerns regarding non-teacher staff compensation.
5.  K. Black, 654 Cedar Blvd. Comment: Support for the agreement.
6.  R. Green, 987 Birch Way. Comment: Inquiry about professional development funds.
7.  S. Brown, 147 Walnut St. Comment: Support for the ratification.
8.  D. Grey, 258 Spruce Dr. Comment: Concerns regarding retirement contributions.
9.  L. Blue, 369 Ash Ct. Comment: Support for the salary increase.
10. C. Red, 741 Maple Ave. Comment: General support for the district.

The public comment period concluded at 7:20 P.M. Total duration recorded as 45 minutes.

Motion to Ratify Contract:
Trustee K. O’Neill made a motion to ratify the Teacher Union Contract Agreement between Northwood Independent School District and the Northwood Education Association.
Trustee D. Chen seconded the motion.

Discussion ensued regarding the specific terms of compensation and the duration of the agreement. Superintendent R. Wilson addressed questions regarding the 4.5% salary increase. Union Representative S. Martinez addressed questions regarding the 3 Years contract term.

Vote on Motion to Ratify:
The President called for a roll call vote.

Trustee M. Thompson: Aye
Trustee L. Garcia: Aye
Trustee K. O’Neill: Aye
Trustee D. Chen: Aye
Trustee A. Johnson: Nay

The vote result was 4-1.
Motion to Ratify passed.

The Board confirmed that the Contract Agreement shall be signed by Superintendent R. Wilson and Union Representative S. Martinez. The Board confirmed the effective date of the contract is 2024-07-01. The Board confirmed the contract term is 3 Years.

PUBLIC COMMENT PERIOD (SPECIAL)

A special public comment period regarding Item 8.1 was opened at 7:25 P.M.
The Board President announced that this period is dedicated solely to the contract ratification.
The duration was set for 45 minutes.
Speakers registered at the desk were provided with a timer.

Speaker 10: J. Resident. Comment regarding the budget.

The public comment period closed at 8:10 P.M.

ADJOURNMENT

Motion to adjourn the meeting was made by Trustee L. Garcia and seconded by Trustee K. O’Neill.
The roll call vote on the adjournment was as follows:

Trustee L. Garcia: Aye

Trustee A. Johnson: Aye

The meeting was adjourned at 8:15 P.M.

J. HARRIS

NORTHWOOD INDEPENDENT SCHOOL DISTRICT
CONTRACT AGREEMENT
AGREEMENT NUMBER: NISD-2024-001
DATE OF EXECUTION: 2023-10-12

THIS AGREEMENT is made and entered into this 12th day of October, 2023, by and between NORTHWOOD INDEPENDENT SCHOOL DISTRICT, a political subdivision of the State of Texas (hereinafter referred to as the "District"), and the NORTHWOOD EDUCATION ASSOCIATION, a labor organization (hereinafter referred to as the "Association").

WHEREAS, the District desires to employ certified personnel to provide educational services to the students of the District; and

WHEREAS, the Association represents the certified employees of the District; and

WHEREAS, the parties desire to establish the terms and conditions of employment for the certified employees;

NOW, THEREFORE, in consideration of the mutual covenants contained herein, the parties agree as follows:

ARTICLE I: DEFINITIONS

1.1 "Certified Employee" shall mean any employee of the District holding a valid teaching certificate issued by the Texas Education Agency.
1.2 "Fiscal Year" shall mean the period from September 1 of any year to August 31 of the following year.
1.3 "Academic Year" shall mean the period from the first day of instruction in September to the last day of instruction in June.
1.4 "Salary Schedule" shall mean the compensation scale attached hereto as Exhibit A.
1.5 "Benefits" shall mean the health, dental, and retirement benefits attached hereto as Exhibit B.

ARTICLE II: TERM

2.1 This Agreement shall become effective on July 1, 2024 (hereinafter referred to as the "Effective Date").
2.2 The term of this Agreement shall be for a period of 3 Years (hereinafter referred to as the "Contract Term").
2.3 The Contract Term shall expire on June 30, 2027.
2.4 Either party may provide written notice of non-renewal at least ninety (90) days prior to the expiration of the Contract Term.
2.5 If no notice of non-renewal is provided, this Agreement shall automatically renew for an additional 1 Year period.

ARTICLE III: COMPENSATION

3.1 Base Salary.
3.1.1 The District shall pay the base salary of each Certified Employee according to the Salary Schedule set forth in Exhibit A.
3.1.2 The Salary Schedule shall reflect a salary increase of 4.5% for all steps on the schedule effective on the Effective Date.
3.1.3 The salary increase shall be prorated for any employee commencing employment after the Effective Date.
3.2 Longevity Pay.
3.2.1 Employees with five (5) or more years of service in the District shall receive a longevity pay increment of $500 per year.
3.2.2 Employees with ten (10) or more years of service in the District shall receive a longevity pay increment of $1,000 per year.
3.3 Stipends.
3.3.1 Stipends for coaching, advising, and department chairperson duties shall be paid in accordance with District Policy 3.10.
3.3.2 Stipends shall be paid on a monthly basis throughout the school year.

ARTICLE IV: WORKING CONDITIONS

4.1 Work Day.
4.1.1 The standard work day for Certified Employees shall be eight (8) hours.
4.1.2 The standard work week shall be five (5) days.
4.2 Class Size.
4.2.1 Class sizes shall not exceed thirty (30) students per classroom for elementary grades.
4.2.2 Class sizes shall not exceed thirty-five (35) students per classroom for secondary grades.
4.3 Planning Time.
4.3.1 Each Certified Employee shall receive at least one (1) planning period per day.
4.3.2 Planning periods shall not be assigned to non-instructional duties.

ARTICLE V: PROFESSIONAL DEVELOPMENT

5.1 District Support.
5.1.1 The District shall allocate funds for professional development activities.
5.1.2 Funds shall be allocated based on the number of Certified Employees employed.
5.2 Leave for Professional Development.
5.2.1 Certified Employees may take up to five (5) days of professional development leave per year.
5.2.2 Professional development leave shall be with full compensation.
5.2.3 Professional development leave must be approved by the Superintendent.

ARTICLE VI: DISPUTE RESOLUTION

6.1 Grievance Procedure.
6.1.1 Grievances shall be processed in accordance with the District Grievance Procedure.
6.1.2 All grievances must be submitted in writing within ten (10) days of the incident.
6.2 Arbitration.
6.2.1 Disputes regarding the interpretation of this Agreement shall be submitted to arbitration.
6.2.2 The arbitrator shall be selected by mutual agreement of the parties.
6.2.3 The decision of the arbitrator shall be final and binding.

ARTICLE VII: TERMINATION

7.1 Termination for Cause.
7.1.1 The District may terminate employment for cause in accordance with state law.
7.1.2 Cause includes insubordination, negligence, or violation of District Policy.
7.2 Termination without Cause.
7.2.1 The District may terminate employment without cause upon payment of severance pay.
7.2.2 Severance pay shall be equal to one (1) month of salary for each year of service.
7.3 Resignation.
7.3.1 Certified Employees shall provide thirty (30) days written notice of resignation.
7.3.2 Failure to provide notice may result in forfeiture of unused sick leave.

ARTICLE VIII: GENERAL PROVISIONS

8.1 Governing Law.
8.1.1 This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of Texas.
8.2 Severability.
8.2.1 If any provision of this Agreement is held invalid, the remainder shall continue in full force.
8.3 Entire Agreement.
8.3.1 This Agreement constitutes the entire agreement between the parties.
8.4 Amendments.
8.4.1 Amendments must be in writing and signed by both parties.
8.5 Notices.
8.5.1 All notices shall be sent via certified mail to the addresses listed in this Agreement.

IN WITNESS WHEREOF, the parties have executed this Agreement on the date first written above.

LEGAL OPINION MEMORANDUM
TO: BOARD OF TRUSTEES, NORTHWOOD INDEPENDENT SCHOOL DISTRICT
FROM: J. MILLER, LEGAL COUNSEL
DATE: 2023-10-10
SUBJECT: LEGAL REVIEW OF CONTRACT RATIFICATION

This memorandum serves as the legal opinion regarding the proposed Contract Agreement between the District and the Northwood Education Association.

1.0 AUTHORITY TO NEGOTIATE
The District has the authority to negotiate and enter into collective bargaining agreements with its employees pursuant to Texas Education Code Section 21.001 et seq.

2.0 COMPLIANCE WITH STATUTE
The proposed agreement complies with all applicable state statutes. The salary increase of 4.5% is within the budgetary limits approved by the Board. The contract term of 3 Years is consistent with standard practice in the region. The effective date of 2024-07-01 aligns with the fiscal year start.

3.0 FUNDING
The necessary funds for the salary increase and benefits are available in the District budget. The financial impact statement attached hereto confirms the availability of funds.

4.0 PROCEDURAL COMPLIANCE
The negotiation process followed all required public notice procedures. The Board meeting on 2023-10-12 provided the required opportunity for public comment. The vote result of 4-1 meets the threshold for adoption.

5.0 RECOMMENDATION
Based on the review, it is the opinion of Legal Counsel that the Contract Agreement is valid and enforceable. The Board is advised to proceed with ratification.

RESPECTFULLY SUBMITTED,
J. MILLER
LEGAL COUNSEL

***

FINANCIAL IMPACT STATEMENT
NORTHWOOD INDEPENDENT SCHOOL DISTRICT
DATE: 2023-10-10
SUBJECT: BUDGETARY IMPACT OF CONTRACT RATIFICATION

1.0 SUMMARY
This statement outlines the financial impact of the proposed Contract Agreement on the District budget for the fiscal year 2024-2025.

2.0 CURRENT SALARY EXPENSE
Current total salary expense for certified employees is $15,000,000.

3.0 PROPOSED SALARY EXPENSE
Proposed total salary expense for certified employees is $15,675,000.

4.0 INCREASE
The proposed increase is $675,000. This represents a 4.5% increase over the current baseline.

5.0 BENEFITS EXPENSE
Current total benefits expense is $3,000,000.
Proposed total benefits expense is $3,135,000.
The proposed increase is $135,000.

6.0 TOTAL IMPACT
The total financial impact is $810,000.
This amount is to be funded through the general fund surplus.

7.0 LONG TERM IMPACT
The 3 Years contract term results in a total commitment of $2,430,000 over the life of the agreement.
This commitment is within the long-term fiscal plan.

8.0 CONCLUSION
The District has the financial capacity to absorb the proposed increase.

RESOLUTION NO. 2023-10-12
NORTHWOOD INDEPENDENT SCHOOL DISTRICT

A RESOLUTION OF THE BOARD OF TRUSTEES OF THE NORTHWOOD INDEPENDENT SCHOOL DISTRICT AUTHORIZING THE RATIFICATION OF THE COLLECTIVE BARGAINING AGREEMENT WITH THE NORTHWOOD EDUCATION ASSOCIATION.

WHEREAS, the Board of Trustees of the Northwood Independent School District (the "Board") has engaged in negotiations with the Northwood Education Association (the "Association") regarding the terms and conditions of employment for certified employees; and

WHEREAS, the parties have reached a tentative agreement on the terms of a new Collective Bargaining Agreement; and

WHEREAS, the Board desires to ratify the agreement and authorize the Superintendent to execute the same;

NOW, THEREFORE, BE IT RESOLVED BY THE BOARD OF TRUSTEES OF THE NORTHWOOD INDEPENDENT SCHOOL DISTRICT AS FOLLOWS:

SECTION 1. RATIFICATION.
The Board hereby ratifies the Collective Bargaining Agreement between the District and the Association, effective July 1, 2024, for a term of 3 Years.

SECTION 2. AUTHORIZATION.
The Board hereby authorizes Superintendent R. Wilson to execute the Agreement on behalf of the District.

SECTION 3. UNIONS REPRESENTATIVE.
The Board acknowledges Union Representative S. Martinez as the signatory for the Association.

SECTION 4. EFFECTIVE DATE.
The Agreement shall become effective on 2024-07-01.

SECTION 5. SALARY INCREASE.
The Agreement includes a salary increase of 4.5%.

SECTION 6. ADOPTION.
This Resolution shall be adopted by a vote of 4-1.

SECTION 7. EFFECTIVE DATE OF RESOLUTION.
This Resolution shall take effect immediately upon adoption.

PASSED AND ADOPTED at a regular meeting of the Board of Trustees of the Northwood Independent School District held on October 12, 2023.

SECTION 3: ZONING COMPLIANCE VERIFICATION MEMO

DEPARTMENT OF PLANNING AND ZONING
CITY OF MILLBROOK
DATE: June 10, 2024
TO: Office of the County Recorder
FROM: Zoning Compliance Officer
RE: Zoning Verification - Parcel 14-05-22-101

This memo serves to confirm the zoning classification and land use compliance for the property located at 1242 Oak Street, Parcel ID 14-05-22-101. The subject property is currently zoned R-1, which designates Single Family Residential use.

The zoning classification R-1 permits the following uses:
1.  Single-family dwelling units.
2.  Home offices (subject to square footage restrictions).
3.  Accessory structures (garages, sheds) not exceeding 1,200 square feet.
4.  Public utilities and infrastructure necessary for residential service.

The property transfer recorded on June 7, 2024, does not require a zoning variance or special use permit. The lot size of 10,890 sq ft meets the minimum frontage requirements for the R-1 district. The acreage of 0.25 acres is consistent with the established density for this sector of the township.

A review of the Millbrook Zoning Code, Section 14.02, indicates that the transfer of ownership does not alter the permitted use of the land. The Grantee, Thompson Residential LLC, has agreed to abide by all covenants associated with the property, including the HOA Agreement.

The survey conducted by Millbrook Surveying Inc on May 15, 2024, confirms that the physical boundaries align with the recorded plat. There are no encroachments or boundary disputes noted in the survey report. The standard utility easement noted in the deed is consistent with existing infrastructure maps held by the Department of Public Works.

No additional zoning approval is required for this transfer. The property remains in compliance with the comprehensive plan for the year 2024.

**SUPERINTENDENT'S REPORT**

Superintendent Marcus Thorne presented a brief overview of district operations for the month of October. He reported that student enrollment remains stable at 14,250 students across 18 campuses. He noted that the fall semester attendance rate stands at 94.2%, a 1.4% increase over the same period last year.

Superintendent Thorne highlighted the progress on the district's capital improvement plan. The renovation of the Northwood High School science wing is 85% complete. The HVAC upgrade for the elementary schools is currently in the bidding phase, with three contractors submitting proposals. The district has also successfully processed the transfer of 450 acres of land in the Northwood West expansion zone, which will accommodate the new middle school construction scheduled for the 2024 fiscal year.

Regarding human resources, the district has hired 22 new teachers to fill vacancies in the mathematics and special education departments. The district's teacher retention rate for the current academic year is 92.5%.

**CONSENT AGENDA**

The following items were listed on the Consent Agenda and will be voted upon as one motion. These items have been reviewed by the Board and the administration and are considered routine and non-controversial.

1.  **Payroll Approval:** Approval of the payroll for the pay period ending October 27, 2023, in the amount of $3,450,212.45.
2.  **Purchase Orders:** Approval of purchase orders totaling $145,000.00 for classroom supplies and technology maintenance.
3.  **Field Trips:** Approval of the following field trips:
    *   Northwood High School: Visit to the Space Center Houston (October 20, 2023) - 145 students, 8 chaperones.
    *   Oak Creek Middle School: Visit to the Dallas Museum of Art (October 24, 2023) - 92 students, 6 chaperones.
    *   Elmwood Elementary: Visit to the Northwood Public Library (October 30, 2023) - 110 students, 10 chaperones.
4.  **Transportation Contracts:** Renewal of the bus maintenance contract with Northwood Fleet Services for the 2023-2024 academic year at a cost of $225,000.00.
5.  **Facility Lease:** Approval of the lease agreement for the temporary modular classroom unit at Northwood High School for the 2023-2024 school year.

**AGENDA ITEM 1: CURRICULUM REVIEW AND ADOPTION**

**Subject:** Review and Recommendation regarding the Advanced STEM Initiative Mathematics Standards.
**Presented by:** Dr. Aris Thorne, Director of Curriculum and Instruction.

Dr. Aris Thorne took the podium to present the Curriculum Review Report regarding the proposed adoption of new mathematics standards for the Advanced STEM Initiative. He introduced the report as a comprehensive analysis of the current curriculum's alignment with state and national standards, specifically focusing on the needs of grades 9 through 12.

Dr. Thorne explained that the Advanced STEM Initiative was originally launched in the 2020-2021 academic year to provide advanced coursework for students interested in engineering, computer science, and physical sciences. Over the past three years, the initiative has seen a 35% increase in student enrollment. However, the current curriculum, which relies on a combination of three different textbook providers, has created inconsistencies in learning outcomes.

The Director presented the findings of the Curriculum Review Report, specifically the section titled "Mathematics Standards." The report detailed a gap analysis between the current district standards and the new proposed standards. The analysis indicated that the current curriculum lacks sufficient depth in calculus preparation for AP Calculus BC and does not adequately integrate computational thinking skills required for modern engineering fields.

Dr. Thorne introduced the vendor proposal submitted by **EduTech Solutions LLC**. He stated that EduTech Solutions LLC has been selected as the primary vendor for the new curriculum package based on a competitive bidding process initiated in June 2023. The vendor proposed a comprehensive suite of materials including digital textbooks, laboratory simulation software, and teacher professional development modules.

The adoption cost for the Advanced STEM Initiative, as outlined in the financial breakdown provided by the vendor, is **$1,250,000**. Dr. Thorne clarified that this figure represents the total cost for the first three years of implementation, covering the purchase of licenses, hardware integration, and the initial training for all 45 teachers assigned to the program. The cost is broken down as follows:
*   Digital Curriculum Licenses (Grades 9-12): $650,000
*   Laboratory Simulation Software: $300,000
*   Teacher Professional Development and Training: $200,000
*   Hardware Integration and Maintenance: $100,000

The report noted that the program is designated for **grade levels 9-12**. The implementation is scheduled to begin in the Spring 2024 semester for the 2024-2025 academic year, with a phased rollout starting with 9th-grade Algebra II and 10th-grade Geometry.

Dr. Thorne presented the rationale for the adoption. He cited data from the Texas Education Agency (TEA) indicating that the Northwood Independent School District ranks in the 75th percentile for mathematics proficiency in high school, but only the 40th percentile for advanced STEM course completion. The new standards proposed by EduTech Solutions LLC are designed to bridge this gap by introducing early exposure to college-level concepts and integrating real-world problem-solving scenarios.

He also highlighted the pedagogical approach of the new curriculum. The materials emphasize inquiry-based learning, where students utilize data from real-world sources to solve complex problems. The curriculum includes a dedicated module on data science and machine learning, which is not currently present in the district's standard mathematics sequence.

Dr. Thorne addressed the financial implications. He noted that the $1,250,000 cost would be drawn from the district's Technology and Innovation Fund, which has a surplus of $1,500,000 for the current fiscal year. No tax levy increase is required for this adoption. The District Finance Officer, Linda Gentry, confirmed the availability of funds in the Technology and Innovation Fund.

The Director of Curriculum and Instruction then opened the floor for a Q&A session with the Board of Trustees regarding the curriculum details.

**Trustee O'Connell** asked about the specific changes to the calculus sequence. Dr. Thorne responded that the new curriculum introduces a "Calculus Readiness" course in 11th grade, which is not currently offered. This course is designed to prepare students for AP Calculus BC in their senior year. The new standards require a 20% increase in problem-solving time during class periods, reducing direct instruction time by approximately 10 minutes per period.

**Trustee Kowalski** inquired about the compatibility of the new software with existing district hardware. Dr. Thorne assured the board that EduTech Solutions LLC has certified the software for compatibility with the current fleet of 2,500 Chromebooks and the 500 desktop computers in the science labs. A pilot program was conducted in the fall of 2023 at Northwood High School with 50 students, and the feedback indicated a 15% improvement in engagement scores compared to the previous curriculum.

**Trustee Martinez** asked about the professional development component. Dr. Thorne explained that the $200,000 allocation covers a 40-hour training program for all participating teachers. The training will be conducted in three phases: an intensive two-week summer institute in June 2024, followed by monthly workshops throughout the fall and spring semesters. The vendor will provide a dedicated curriculum coach for each high school campus to support teachers during the transition.

**Trustee Miller** raised a question regarding the long-term sustainability of the program. He asked if there are any recurring costs beyond the initial three-year license. Dr. Thorne clarified that the $1,250,000 cost includes a three-year license renewal. After the three-year period, the district would need to budget for an annual renewal fee, estimated at $250,000. This would be a recurring line item in the technology budget.

**Student Trustee Chen** asked about the impact on students who are not currently enrolled in the Advanced STEM Initiative. Dr. Thorne explained that the new standards are designed to be modular. While the core curriculum is targeted at the Advanced STEM Initiative, the district plans to create a "STEM Foundation" track for general education students that incorporates the same inquiry-based learning methods but at a lower intensity. This would allow for a broader application of the new pedagogical tools across the district.

Following the Q&A, Dr. Aris Thorne concluded his presentation. He recommended that the Board of Trustees adopt the new Mathematics Standards for the Advanced STEM Initiative, approve the vendor selection of **EduTech Solutions LLC**, and authorize the expenditure of **$1,250,000** for the implementation of the program for **grades 9-12**.

**AGENDA ITEM 2: PUBLIC COMMENT SUBMISSIONS**

The Board moved to the Public Comment section of the meeting. The Superintendent reminded the audience of the public comment guidelines: each speaker is limited to three minutes, comments must be relevant to the agenda items, and disruptive behavior will not be tolerated.

The Clerk of the Board, Sarah Jenkins, announced that the district had received a total of **127 public comment submissions** regarding the Curriculum Feedback section of the agenda. The submissions were received via email, online portal, and physical mail between October 25, 2023, and November 7, 2023. The Clerk noted that the sentiment of the submissions was **Mixed**.

A summary of the submissions was presented by the Office of Communications:
*   **Supportive Comments:** 62 submissions (48.8%)
*   **Opposing Comments:** 45 submissions (35.4%)
*   **Neutral/Questions:** 20 submissions (15.8%)

The supportive comments generally praised the district for taking action to modernize the mathematics curriculum and align with industry standards. Many parents expressed gratitude for the focus on data science and the inclusion of real-world problem solving.

The opposing comments raised concerns regarding the cost of the program, the potential for increased homework load, and the need for more teacher training before implementation. Some parents expressed concern that the new curriculum might disadvantage students who are not naturally inclined toward advanced mathematics.

The Clerk read the following excerpts from the public comment submissions:

**Submission 001:** "We strongly support the adoption of the Advanced STEM Initiative. The current curriculum is outdated and does not prepare our children for the workforce. The investment of $1,250,000 is a drop in the bucket compared to the long-term benefits of a mathematically proficient workforce. - Parent of two Northwood High School students."

**Submission 045:** "I am concerned about the cost. $1,250,000 is a significant amount of money that could be used to hire more counselors or improve the arts program. We need to ensure that the vendor, EduTech Solutions LLC, is the best choice before spending this much. - Concerned Parent."

**Submission 089:** "The curriculum looks promising, but I worry about the transition. Our teachers need more time to learn the new software. I hope the professional development is robust enough to support them. - Teacher at Oak Creek Middle School."

**Submission 112:** "I am a student in the Advanced STEM Initiative. I love the current program, but I think the new standards will make it even better. I want to learn more about coding and data analysis. - Student, Grade 11."

**Submission 127:** "I have three children in the district. I am mixed on this. I want the best for my kids, but I am worried about the pressure on them. Please ensure that the new curriculum does not increase stress levels. - Parent of three."

Following the reading of the summaries, the Board opened the floor for live public comment.

**Speaker 1: Mr. James Peterson, District 2 Resident**
Mr. Peterson took the podium. He stated that he had reviewed the Curriculum Review Report and had concerns about the specific vendor selection. He asked if the bidding process had been fully transparent and if other vendors were considered. He noted that the vendor, EduTech Solutions LLC, had a contract with a neighboring district, but he was unaware of the performance metrics in that district. He requested a copy of the contract terms before the vote.

**Response:** Superintendent Thorne stated that the contract terms are public record and are available for review in the Superintendent's office. He confirmed that four vendors were considered, and EduTech Solutions LLC was selected based on the highest score in the technical evaluation and the most competitive pricing.

**Speaker 2: Mrs. Linda Gomez, Northwood High School Parent**
Mrs. Gomez expressed strong support for the initiative. She stated that her son had struggled with the current curriculum and that the new standards would provide the hands-on learning he needed. She thanked the Board for prioritizing STEM education.

**Speaker 3: Mr. Robert Wu, Northwood High School Student**
Mr. Wu spoke on behalf of the student body. He mentioned that many students are interested in the data science module but are concerned about the workload. He requested that the Board ensure that the new curriculum does not negatively impact other subjects.

**Speaker 4: Ms. Sarah Miller, District 1 Resident**
Ms. Miller raised a concern about the equity of the program. She asked if the new curriculum would be available to all students or only those in the Advanced STEM Initiative. She requested that the Board consider a plan to integrate the new teaching methods into the general education curriculum to ensure all students benefit.

**Speaker 5: Mr. David Johnson, Parent**
Mr. Johnson questioned the long-term sustainability of the program. He asked if the district had a plan for the recurring costs after the three-year license period. He expressed concern that the district might have to cut other programs in the future to maintain this one.

**Response:** Dr. Aris Thorne addressed the equity concern, stating that the district is currently developing a "STEM Foundation" track that will use the same inquiry-based methods for general education students. He also confirmed that the Finance Department is preparing a five-year financial projection to ensure the recurring costs are manageable.

**Speaker 6: Mrs. Emily Carter, Teacher**
Mrs. Carter, a mathematics teacher at Northwood High School, spoke in support of the new curriculum. She stated that the current materials are outdated and that the new software would significantly enhance her ability to teach. She thanked the Board for investing in teacher professional development.

**Speaker 7: Mr. Thomas Brown, Parent**
Mr. Brown expressed opposition to the cost. He argued that the district should focus on fixing the physical infrastructure of the schools before investing in new software. He asked the Board to reconsider the budget allocation.

**Speaker 8: Ms. Rachel Green, Student**
Ms. Green, a junior at Northwood High School, spoke about the importance of technology in the classroom. She stated that the new curriculum would help her prepare for college and that the data science module was exactly what she was looking for.

**Speaker 9: Mr. John Smith, Resident**
Mr. Smith asked for clarification on the specific grade levels affected. He wanted to know if the new curriculum would replace the current curriculum entirely or if it would be an add-on.

**Response:** Dr. Thorne clarified that the new curriculum will replace the current curriculum for the Advanced STEM Initiative track. It is not an add-on but a comprehensive replacement.

**Speaker 10: Mrs. Anne White, Parent**
Mrs. White expressed concern about the transition period. She asked how the district would support students who might struggle during the switch.

**Response:** Superintendent Thorne assured the Board that the district would provide tutoring and support services during the transition period. He also mentioned that the vendor would provide a dedicated support line for parents and students.

**Speaker 11: Mr. Michael Davis, Teacher**
Mr. Davis, a science teacher, spoke about the potential for cross-disciplinary learning. He suggested that the new mathematics curriculum could be integrated with the science curriculum to create a more cohesive STEM experience.

**Speaker 12: Mrs. Jennifer Lee, Parent**
Mrs. Lee asked about the impact on the college entrance exam scores. She wanted to know if the new curriculum was designed to improve SAT and ACT scores.

**Response:** Dr. Thorne stated that the curriculum is aligned with the standards required for AP Calculus and other college-level exams, and the district expects to see an improvement in these scores over time.

**Speaker 13: Mr. William Taylor, Resident**
Mr. Taylor asked about the environmental impact of the new software. He wanted to know if the digital curriculum would reduce the need for paper textbooks.

**Response:** Dr. Thorne confirmed that the digital curriculum would significantly reduce paper usage, aligning with the district's sustainability goals.

**Speaker 14: Mrs. Patricia Anderson, Parent**
Mrs. Anderson expressed support for the initiative but asked about the timeline for implementation. She wanted to know if the program would start in the fall or the spring.

**Response:** Dr. Thorne clarified that the program would begin in the Spring 2024 semester for the 2024-2025 academic year.

**Speaker 15: Mr. Richard Wilson, Teacher**
Mr. Wilson spoke about the need for ongoing professional development. He emphasized that one-time training would not be sufficient for a program of this magnitude.

**Response:** Superintendent Thorne reiterated that the $200,000 allocation covers a 40-hour training program with monthly workshops throughout the year.

**Speaker 16: Mrs. Susan Clark, Parent**
